name: Platform-Neutral Authentication Framework
consequences: Establishing open-standard authentication models reduces reliance on proprietary platform signals but risks underestimating the complexity of integrating with existing MitID infrastructure. This could delay adoption if stakeholders perceive the framework as incompatible with current security mandates.
options: Develop FIDO2/WebAuthn-based authentication without platform-specific integrity checks
Partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics in hardware-token deployment
Create a browser-based fallback authentication layer using decentralized identifiers
review: Technical feasibility vs. institutional inertia. Weakness: The options understate the regulatory hurdles of redefining 'trust' in authentication without platform signals.

name: Regulatory Resilience Framing
consequences: Reframing dependency risks as resilience imperatives could secure policy support but may provoke pushback from stakeholders benefiting from the status quo. This could slow consensus-building if technical evidence is perceived as speculative.
options: Leverage procurement mandates to require supplier diversification in identity contracts
Align fallback requirements with EU eIDAS2 continuity-of-service clauses
Publish risk assessments linking platform policies to public-sector service disruptions
review: Policy advocacy vs. technical pragmatism. Weakness: The options neglect the political capital required to shift narratives from privacy concerns to systemic risk management.

name: Coalition-Driven Standards Pressure
consequences: Building cross-sector coalitions strengthens advocacy but risks diluting technical focus if stakeholder priorities diverge. This could fragment efforts if consensus on fallback mechanisms remains elusive.
options: Form an industry-academia task force to audit platform dependency surfaces
Engage privacy advocates to co-develop fallback authentication use cases
Create a public-private working group to model contingency scenarios
review: Collaboration vs. specialization. Weakness: The options fail to address how to manage conflicting interests within the coalition.

name: Procurement-Linked Technical Demonstration
consequences: Tying demonstrators to procurement timelines increases accountability but may force premature simplification of complex technical challenges. This could compromise the demonstration's credibility if scope is overly constrained.
options: Design demonstrators to meet AltID's minimum compliance thresholds
Use GrapheneOS as a reference implementation for privacy-respecting fallback paths
Simulate platform-neutral authentication in a sandboxed public-sector environment
review: Demonstration scope vs. procurement deadlines. Weakness: The options do not specify how to handle technical debt from non-production demonstrators.

name: EU-Level Interoperability Advocacy
consequences: Aligning with European Digital Identity Wallet frameworks could amplify influence but may require compromising on national specificity. This could dilute the project's focus on Denmark's unique sovereignty concerns.
options: Propose platform-neutral authentication as an EU-wide interoperability standard
Participate in eIDAS2 technical working groups to shape fallback requirements
Leverage EU funding mechanisms to support regional identity infrastructure pilots
review: National priorities vs. supranational alignment. Weakness: The options overlook the bureaucratic complexity of EU policy integration.

name: Stakeholder Coalition Mobilization
consequences: Mobilizing cross-sector coalitions strengthens advocacy influence but risks diluting focus on technical priorities due to competing stakeholder interests. A fragmented coalition may weaken credibility with institutional decision-makers.
options: Mobilize cross-sector stakeholder coalitions through targeted policy workshops and joint advocacy campaigns
Leverage academic institutions as neutral mediators to bridge gaps between public-sector stakeholders and private-sector technology providers
Establish a civil-society-led oversight body to coordinate stakeholder engagement and ensure alignment with digital sovereignty goals
review: Balancing coalition breadth vs. technical focus. Weakness: Options overlook the need for structured governance mechanisms within the coalition.

name: Alternative Technical Ecosystem Integration
consequences: Integrating open-source or decentralized identity protocols creates technical alternatives but may face resistance from entrenched platform vendors. Early adoption risks compatibility challenges with existing infrastructure.
options: Adopt FIDO2/WebAuthn-based authentication models with minimal reliance on proprietary platform APIs
Develop a Linux-focused identity verification layer using GrapheneOS as a reference implementation
Pilot hardware-token-based authentication for high-security public services to reduce OS dependency
review: Technical innovation vs. institutional inertia. Weakness: Options fail to address potential conflicts with existing procurement contracts.

name: Legal Framework Advocacy
consequences: Proposing legal mandates for platform-neutral access requirements could institutionalize change but may face judicial or legislative delays. Overly prescriptive language risks limiting future technical flexibility.
options: Draft legislation requiring public-sector identity systems to include certified fallback authentication paths
Advocate for amendments to eIDAS2 that explicitly address platform dependency risks in digital identity frameworks
Leverage EU Digital Identity Wallet guidelines to push for mandatory interoperability clauses in Danish procurement
review: Regulatory ambition vs. political feasibility. Weakness: Options do not account for potential legal challenges from platform vendors.

name: International Standards Alignment
consequences: Aligning with global identity standards enhances credibility but may require compromises on sovereignty priorities. Over-reliance on external frameworks could delay localized implementation.
options: Engage with W3C and IETF to shape open authentication standards that prioritize platform neutrality
Adopt ISO/IEC 24760-1 as a baseline for evaluating fallback authentication mechanisms
Collaborate with Nordic counterparts to create a regional identity interoperability framework
review: Global alignment vs. local control. Weakness: Options lack specific strategies for balancing international standards with Danish regulatory needs.

name: Public-Private Partnership Formation
consequences: Forming partnerships with private-sector entities accelerates resource access but may compromise independence. Conflicting commercial interests could undermine technical neutrality.
options: Create a public-private consortium to co-develop open-source identity verification tools
Partner with cybersecurity firms to conduct third-party audits of platform-neutral authentication models
Establish a shared risk-mitigation fund with private stakeholders to cover fallback system development costs
review: Collaboration vs. autonomy. Weakness: Options do not address potential conflicts of interest in partnership structures.

name: Interoperability Protocol Development
consequences: Establishing open standards for cross-platform authentication could reduce vendor lock-in but may face resistance from entrenched providers. This could delay adoption if existing systems lack backward compatibility.
options: Adopt FIDO2 as the baseline for all public-sector authentication systems
Implement mandatory encryption standards for data transmission in identity protocols
Create a standardized framework for third-party security audits
review: Balances technical standardization vs. vendor resistance. Weakness: Overlooks potential delays in legacy system integration.

name: Procurement Mandate Development
consequences: Instituting platform-neutral procurement requirements could force vendor compliance but might slow down contract approvals if stakeholders resist policy changes. This could create short-term operational friction.
options: Institute mandatory platform-neutral requirements in all public-sector contracts
Create a tiered procurement system that prioritizes supplier diversity
Establish a certification body to validate non-platform-dependent solutions
review: Balances regulatory enforcement vs. vendor flexibility. Weakness: Ignores potential procurement bottlenecks during transition periods.

name: Cross-Sector Collaboration
consequences: Forming multi-stakeholder partnerships could accelerate innovation but may dilute focus if conflicting priorities emerge. This requires sustained coordination across technical, policy, and commercial domains.
options: Form a multi-stakeholder task force to align technical and policy goals
Initiate joint research initiatives with academic institutions on alternative authentication methods
Develop a shared infrastructure repository for open-source identity solutions
review: Balances stakeholder alignment vs. project focus. Weakness: Underestimates coordination complexity in diverse coalitions.

name: Security Protocol Standardization
consequences: Enforcing stricter security protocols could enhance resilience but might increase implementation costs for public agencies. This could create budgetary constraints if resources are not allocated strategically.
options: Adopt FIDO2 as the baseline for all public-sector authentication systems
Implement mandatory encryption standards for data transmission in identity protocols
Create a standardized framework for third-party security audits
review: Balances security rigor vs. operational cost. Weakness: Fails to address resource allocation challenges for public agencies.

name: Public Sector Adoption Incentives
consequences: Offering financial incentives for platform-neutral solutions could drive adoption but may face budgetary constraints if funding is not secured. This could limit scalability without long-term fiscal commitments.
options: Offer financial incentives for public agencies to adopt platform-neutral solutions
Develop a certification program that grants preferential treatment in future contracts
Launch a public awareness campaign highlighting the benefits of digital sovereignty
review: Balances incentive design vs. fiscal responsibility. Weakness: Overlooks long-term funding sustainability for adoption programs.
